museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: The month before the N64 officially reached Europe: Mario Kart 64 accelerated in North America, Turok loomed, and UK shelves waited for a new console war chapter.
museum.curatorNote: February is a hinge month. The strongest dated anchor is Mario Kart 64's North American release; UK content is mostly launch build-up rather than street-date certainty.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: A month of acceleration: racing abroad, marketing at home, and one last stretch before the European N64 launch.

items.title: Mario Kart 64 reaches North America
items.body: Nintendo's kart sequel arrived in North America, sharpening the N64's multiplayer identity before British players had the console officially on shelves.
items.sourceNote: Mario Kart 64 references list North America as February 10, 1997; Europe follows in June.

items.title: Turok becomes the imminent N64 showpiece
items.body: Acclaim's dinosaur shooter was about to become one of the first third-party tests of the N64's early identity: foggy, violent, expensive, and technically fascinating.
items.sourceNote: Turok is tied to early March 1997 by multiple release references; February is pre-release context.

items.title: UK magazines prepare the console launch
items.body: N64 launch coverage moved from speculation to buying advice. The questions were practical: price, cartridges, and which game justified the leap.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: February's notable games are best read as North American momentum and UK anticipation.

items.title: Mario Kart 64
items.body: North American release. In the UK, it still lived in preview pages and import envy.

items.title: Diablo
items.body: Still fresh enough to dominate PC evenings, especially for players discovering Battle.net and loot obsession after launch.
items.sourceNote: January release context; included for February momentum.

items.title: N64 launch shelf previews
items.body: Super Mario 64, Pilotwings 64, and Shadows of the Empire were treated as the imminent UK starter set.
items.sourceNote: Computing History lists the three European launch games.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: The question was not whether the N64 was powerful. It was whether cartridges and price felt worth it.

items.title: Cartridge confidence and anxiety
items.body: Nintendo's cartridge format promised speed and durability, but UK buyers could already see the price difference against PlayStation CD-ROM shelves.
items.sourceNote: Included as retail/platform context around the European N64 launch.

items.title: PC networking becomes less exotic
items.body: Diablo and QuakeWorld made multiplayer networking feel more plausible, even if most homes still treated a modem session as an appointment.
items.sourceNote: Battle.net and QuakeWorld were active by early 1997.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: The shelf acted like a launch countdown.

items.title: N64 launch previews
items.headline: Nintendo 64 buying advice
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed N64 magazine-style cover image standing in for February 1997.
items.curatorNote: The magazine became a pre-order companion: screenshots, verdicts, and the question of whether Mario was enough.
items.body: The magazine became a pre-order companion: screenshots, verdicts, and the question of whether Mario was enough.

items.title: PC dungeon talk
items.headline: Diablo, networks and the family computer
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed PC magazine-style cover image standing in for February 1997.
items.curatorNote: PC coverage made Diablo feel like both a game and a warning about lost evenings.
items.body: PC coverage made Diablo feel like both a game and a warning about lost evenings.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Online play was still a thing you announced to the household.

items.title: Battle.net becomes a destination
items.body: Diablo gave PC owners a reason to connect repeatedly, not just browse once.

items.title: N64 multiplayer was local by design
items.body: Before the console even launched in Europe, its four controller ports promised a different kind of network: friends on a carpet.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: February was mostly anticipation, but anticipation had a shape.

items.title: The N64 was almost touchable
items.body: By now the console was not rumour. It was a date, a price, a launch shelf, and a decision.

items.title: PC games felt bottomless
items.body: Diablo made the family computer feel less like a homework machine and more like a trapdoor.
